title: Target write slowdown and catch-up
summary: Explains how target Redis write latency or disconnection constrains processor throughput, increases backlog, and can lead to backpressure.

question_patterns: What happens if the target is slow?
Why does target write latency increase backlog?
Why is RDI backpressured when the target is disconnected?
How does RDI catch up after target recovery?

nodes.label: Target connection health
nodes.description: Whether the stream processor can write to the Redis target database.

nodes.label: Target write latency
nodes.description: The time needed to write transformed records to the Redis target database.

nodes.label: Processor throughput
nodes.description: The rate at which records complete read, transform, write, and acknowledgement steps.

nodes.label: RDI stream backlog
nodes.description: Captured events waiting in RDI streams to be processed, written to the target, and acknowledged.

nodes.label: Stream event latency
nodes.description: The age of the oldest unprocessed event in each RDI data stream.

nodes.label: RDI database memory pressure
nodes.description: Memory pressure from staged change events and RDI state in the Redis Enterprise cluster.

nodes.label: Backpressure level
nodes.description: The degree to which RDI slows or stops incoming data to protect the streams and target side of the pipeline.

nodes.label: Target data freshness
nodes.description: How up to date the Redis target database is compared with the source database.

edges.condition: The target Redis database is reachable and accepting writes.
edges.summary: A healthy target connection allows transformed records to be written and completed.

edges.condition: Target writes are on the critical path for completed processing.
edges.summary: Slow target writes reduce the rate at which the processor can complete batches.

edges.condition: Completed throughput exceeds stream append rate.
edges.summary: When processing completes faster than events arrive, the RDI stream backlog falls.
A larger backlog tends to increase the age of the oldest unprocessed stream event.
A growing stream backlog consumes more memory in the RDI database.

edges.condition: RDI detects that stream growth could exhaust available memory.
edges.summary: RDI increases backpressure when backlog and memory pressure threaten the pipeline.
Higher stream event latency means the Redis target is less fresh.
Higher completed throughput keeps the Redis target closer to the source.

feedback_loops.label: Catch-up after target recovery
feedback_loops.summary: After the target recovers, processor throughput can exceed incoming event rate. Backlog and stream latency fall, memory pressure eases, and backpressure can release.

causal_chains.question: What happens when the target Redis database is slow or disconnected?
causal_chains.summary: Target slowdown reduces completed processing throughput. If events continue arriving, backlog and event latency rise, and backpressure may activate.

diagnostic_hints.interpretation: The target side is likely limiting pipeline completion rate.

answering_guidance: Reason downstream-to-upstream when target writes are slow.
Target disconnection or slow writes reduce completed throughput before they increase backlog.
Backpressure caused by target slowdown is a protective response to backlog and memory pressure.
